title: Estate Developers Seek More Govt Support as Nigeria’s Housing Deficit Exceeds 20m Units
description: Estate Developers Seek More Govt Support as Nigeria’s Housing Deficit Exceeds 20m Units  THISDAYLIVE
content: Friday, 19th June, 2026 Home Business Politics Nigeria International Africa Rest of the World Health & Wellbeing Education Life & Style Sport Editorial Backpage Breaking News Govs Demand Constitutional Safeguards for State Police 24 hours ago UK Court Clears Ex-Minister, Alison-Madueke, of All Graft Charges 1 day ago Eyesan Highlights Infrastructure, Asset Integrity as Key Constraints in Oil Industry 1 day ago INEC: 1,059,360 Registered Voters to Elect New Governor for Ekiti State June 20 1 day ago Latest Headlines Africa and France: From Colonial Shadows to Partnership of Equals Ovie Omo-Agege and Delta Central’s Politics of Defiance Why Effective Communication Matters in Govt Oyebanji: Tinubu Made Governance Easier for Me in Ekiti Estate Developers Seek More Govt Support as Nigeria’s Housing Deficit Exceeds 20m Units Nigeria | 6 hours ago Shares Share Post Email Share Share • Say building costs, funding constraints, obstacles to affordable housing delivery • Seek legislation to improve professionalism and investor confidence Sunday Aborisade in Abuja The Federal Capital Territory (FCT) chapter of Real Estate Developers Association of Nigeria (REDAN) said Nigeria’s housing deficit had exceeded 20 million units, urging the federal government to provide stronger support and incentives to developers to accelerate housing delivery and improve affordability. Vice Chairman of REDAN, FCT Chapter, Mr. Amos Gbadewole, stated this in Abuja while addressing participants at the National Youth Entrepreneurship and Empowerment Programme (YEEP) 2026, where he spoke on entrepreneurship, housing development, and opportunities for young Nigerians. Gbadewole, who is also Managing Director and Chief Executive Officer of Crown Luxury Properties, lamented the widening gap between housing demand and supply. He underscored the need for greater collaboration between government and private sector operators to expand access to affordable housing. “The housing deficit in Nigeria is estimated at over 20 million units for a population of more than 250 million people. That is a huge gap that must be bridged,” he said. He identified limited access to affordable land, inadequate financing, shortage of skilled professionals, and the rising cost of building materials as some of the major challenges confronting estate developers across the country. He said, “One of the major challenges facing developers is access to affordable land and access to professionals. “We also have funding challenges because many developers do not have access to capital to acquire land and build estates. “The increasing cost of building materials is another major concern.” Gbadewole acknowledged existing government interventions in the housing sector but maintained that additional measures were required to lower construction costs and make homes more affordable. “We need government regulations and incentives that can help reduce the cost of building materials. If government comes to the aid of developers
summary: The article highlights Nigeria's housing deficit exceeding 20m units, which means that Lagos real estate market will face increased demand for affordable housing, potentially affecting Mixta Africa's strategic priorities and active projects, particularly in the affordable housing segment.

mixta_relevance.direct_impact: This affects our strategic priority of shifting new-sales mix toward higher-margin / faster-converting segments, as the increased demand for affordable housing may hinder our premium segment growth.
mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: The article's emphasis on building costs, funding constraints, and obstacles to affordable housing delivery may impact our pricing and costs, particularly in the affordable housing segment.
mixta_relevance.strategic_opportunity: The increased demand for affordable housing presents an opportunity for Mixta Africa to scale its affordable housing offerings, particularly in Lagos New Town (LNT), and potentially partner with healthcare operators for the proposed Lakowe Wellness and Medical Center.
mixta_relevance.risk_flag: The article's focus on government support for the real estate sector may pose a risk to our execution, particularly if government policies or regulations hinder our ability to deliver affordable housing projects.

title: Renewed Hope Housing Initiative Gains Momentum as FHA Revives FESTAC
description: Renewed Hope Housing Initiative Gains Momentum as FHA Revives FESTAC  nigeriahousingmarket.com
content: FHA Targets FESTAC Revival as Affordable Housing Push Expands Nationwide housinglatest local news May 24 Written By Ayomide Fiyinfunoluwa FHA Seeks to Expand Affordable Housing Access Through FESTAC Redevelopment The Federal Housing Authority (FHA) has announced renewed efforts to restore FESTAC Town and expand affordable housing delivery across Nigeria as part of the Federal Government’s broader housing and urban development agenda. The Managing Director and Chief Executive Officer of the FHA, Oyetunde Oladimeji Ojo, said the agency is repositioning itself to address Nigeria’s housing deficit through infrastructure rehabilitation, strategic partnerships with state governments and increased access to home ownership under the Renewed Hope initiative. / You Might Also Like / The 2026 Dangote Refinery Blueprint: Location, IPO, and Global Energy Impact The Lagos-Calabar Coastal Highway: Transforming Nigeria’s Infrastructure Landscape Nigeria’s Housing Deficit: How Big Is the Problem in 2026? Mistakes First-Time Property Investors Make in Nigeria According to Ojo, the FHA is moving beyond previous operational and funding limitations by pursuing nationwide housing projects designed to improve affordability and expand housing supply. FHA Repositions for Affordable Housing Delivery The FHA stated that the current administration’s housing strategy is centred on collaboration with state governments, infrastructure renewal and lower development costs. Ojo explained that several governors have provided land support and absorbed compensation and tax-related obligations, reducing project costs for developers and improving affordability for prospective homeowners. The agency estimates that Nigeria’s housing deficit ranges between 20 million and 28 million units, reinforcing the urgency for large-scale public and private sector housing interventions. While analysts continue to debate the precise deficit figure, housing experts broadly agree that supply shortages remain one of the country’s most significant urban development challenges. Ojo stated that the FHA has adopted a decentralised approach to housing delivery, engaging directly with state governments rather than relying solely on federal-level planning. He noted that land administration under the Land Use Act makes state-level cooperation critical to accelerating project implementation. FESTAC Redevelopment Returns to Focus A major component of the FHA’s current strategy is the rehabilitation and redevelopment of FESTAC Town in Lagos, one of Nigeria’s most prominent public housing estates. Originally developed for the Second World Black and African Festival of Arts and Culture in 1977, FESTAC has faced decades of infrastructure deterioration, weak maintenance systems and urban management challenges. The FHA acknowledged that years of limited funding and administrative constraints contributed to the estate’s decline. The authority disclosed that rehabilitation work has already commenced in collaboration wit
summary: The renewed efforts by the Federal Housing Authority (FHA) to restore FESTAC Town and expand affordable housing delivery nationwide may boost demand for affordable housing in Lagos, benefiting Mixta Africa's premium Lakowe Lakes products and LNT commercial plots.

mixta_relevance.direct_impact: The FHA's efforts may increase demand for affordable housing in Lagos, which could indirectly benefit Mixta Africa's premium products and LNT commercial plots by shifting the new-sales mix toward higher-margin segments.
mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: The FHA's partnerships with state governments and increased access to home ownership may lead to regulatory changes or changes in the competitive landscape, affecting Mixta Africa's costs, pricing, or regulatory environment.
mixta_relevance.strategic_opportunity: The FHA's initiative creates an opportunity for Mixta Africa to partner with the agency or leverage its efforts to expand its affordable housing offerings and tap into the growing demand for affordable housing in Lagos.
mixta_relevance.risk_flag: The FHA's efforts may also increase competition in the affordable housing segment, threatening Mixta Africa's market share if it fails to adapt and innovate its offerings.

title: FG moves to unlock affordable housing through cooperatives, digital finance
description: FG moves to unlock affordable housing through cooperatives, digital finance  Vanguard News
content: By Joseph Erunke, Abuja The Federal Government on Tuesday unveiled fresh plans to drive affordable housing delivery through cooperative societies, digital finance and strategic partnerships, declaring that conventional mortgage systems alone can no longer solve Nigeria’s housing crisis. Speaking at the 2026 Cooperative Housing Summit Africa (CHOSA) in Abuja, top government officials and stakeholders said millions of low and middle-income Nigerians remained locked out of home ownership due to rising housing costs, weak financing structures and limited access to mortgages. Minister of State for Agriculture and Food Security, Senator Aliyu Sabi Abdullahi, disclosed that the government was pushing reforms to reposition the cooperative sector as a major vehicle for affordable housing and financial inclusion. Abdullahi revealed plans for the establishment of a Cooperative Bank of Nigeria designed to provide accessible financing for cooperative housing schemes, mortgages, infrastructure development and community projects. According to him, the proposed bank would operate under a cooperative ownership structure and focus on underserved Nigerians, especially workers in the informal sector. “The cooperative sector is a sleeping giant. Through cooperative systems, we can democratise access to housing, expand financial inclusion and build resilient communities,” he said. The minister also said government was digitising cooperative operations nationwide to improve transparency, financial management, access to credit and investor confidence. He explained that digital finance platforms would simplify mortgage repayment systems, housing project monitoring and cooperative member verification. Earlier, the Minister of Housing and Urban Development, Dr. Muttaqha Darma, represented by Samuel Pemi, Director of Public Buildings and Housing Development, said cooperative housing remained one of the most practical solutions to Africa’s growing housing deficit. He noted that technology-driven finance would play a major role in improving mortgage administration and housing access for citizens in both formal and informal sectors. Darma reaffirmed the ministry’s commitment to strengthening housing finance systems and supporting sustainable urban development across Nigeria and Africa. Convener of the summit and Founder of Nigeria Integrated Social Housing Cooperative Ltd. (NISH), Dr. Saheed Adelakun, faulted the traditional Public-Private Partnership (PPP) model, insisting that affordable housing delivery must now embrace a “Public-Private-People Partnership” approach. According to him, intended homeowners must be directly involved in housing projects for affordability to be achieved. “We need houses that low and middle-income earners can truly afford. Government, developers, cooperatives and homebuyers must work together,” he said. President of the Cooperative Federation of Nigeria (CFN), Mrs Hannatu Mershak, said the federation currently supervises more than 50,000 cooperat
summary: The Federal Government's plan to drive affordable housing delivery through cooperative societies, digital finance, and strategic partnerships will increase demand for affordable housing in Lagos, benefiting Mixta Africa's Lakowe Lakes and Lagos New Town (LNT) projects.

mixta_relevance.direct_impact: This initiative aligns with Mixta Africa's strategic priority of shifting new-sales mix toward higher-margin / faster-converting segments, particularly premium Lakowe Lakes products and LNT commercial plots.
mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: The increased demand for affordable housing may lead to higher land costs and construction prices, affecting Mixta Africa's costs and pricing strategy.
mixta_relevance.strategic_opportunity: Mixta Africa can leverage its existing projects and expertise to capitalize on the government's initiative, potentially increasing its market share and revenue.
mixta_relevance.risk_flag: The government's plan may lead to increased competition for Mixta Africa, particularly from other developers with experience in cooperative housing schemes.

title: Policy Alignment is key to Unlocking Nigeria’s Housing Potential, says Ryan
description: Policy Alignment is key to Unlocking Nigeria’s Housing Potential, says Ryan  The Guardian Nigeria News
content: Policy Alignment is key to Unlocking Nigeria’s Housing Potential, says Ryan By : David Meshioye Date: 26 April 2026 3:35pm WAT Share : Follow Us on Google Discover CEO of Zeeks Homes Ltd, Ezekiel Ryan CEO of Zeeks Homes Ltd, Ezekiel Ryan CEO of Zeeks Homes Ltd, Ezekiel Ryan has outlined a forward-looking vision for Nigeria’s real estate sector, emphasizing that despite current challenges, the country remains one of the most promising frontiers for housing development globally. Ezekiel Ryan noted that while luxury real estate continues to perform as a hedge against inflation, the true opportunity lies in addressing the underserved mid-income segment. Developers who adopt efficient designs, embrace cost control, and introduce flexible payment options are best positioned to succeed in this evolving market. A major opportunity highlighted is incremental housing, which allows buyers to build and expand homes in phases. This model aligns with the financial realities of many Nigerians and offers a practical path to homeownership. Additionally, the adoption of alternative building materials, modular construction methods, and local sourcing could significantly reduce costs and limit exposure to foreign exchange volatility. Technology is also expected to play a transformative role. Improved systems for property management, land verification, and sales transparency can enhance trust and efficiency across the sector. Ryan stressed that digital tools will be critical in modernizing operations and attracting investment. Geographically, the outskirts of major cities are emerging as the next growth frontier. As central districts become saturated and unaffordable, peripheral areas with improving infrastructure are witnessing rapid appreciation. The development of self-sustaining communities in these regions offers a scalable solution to urban housing shortages. However, unlocking these opportunities depends on addressing systemic barriers. Ezekiel reiterated that policy inconsistency, bureaucratic delays, and limited access to financing continue to hinder progress. Without alignment between government policies, financial systems, and private sector execution, the sector’s full potential will remain unrealized. Encouragingly, initiatives such as recent tax adjustments and land reform programs indicate a shift toward addressing these issues. Ezekiel Ryan believes that sustained policy improvements, particularly in land administration and developer incentives, could dramatically increase housing supply and affordability. Zeeks Homes Ltd emphasized that its approach goes beyond construction, focusing on navigating the broader ecosystem to deliver sustainable solutions. The company maintains that with the right reforms and continued innovation, Nigeria can bridge its housing gap and make homeownership accessible to millions. The message from Ezekiel is clear: the demand exists, the talent is available, and the capital is within reach. What Nigeria needs now is coordin

title: How demand for high-end property fuels investment in first-tier cities
description: How demand for high-end property fuels investment in first-tier cities  The Guardian Nigeria News
content: How demand for high-end property fuels investment in first-tier cities By : Victor Gbonegun Date: 16 March 2026 4:18am WAT Share : Follow Us on Google Discover Amid increased appetite for prime urban properties, developers are increasingly investing in that segment of the real estate sector, which attracts affluent local investors, expatriates and Nigerians in the diaspora. Experts argue that strategic government intervention, adoption of innovative construction methods, and enhanced transparency in property ownership could help balance profitability with broader housing needs, VICTOR GBONEGUN reports. Nigeria’s first-tier cities are witnessing a surge in the development of high-end properties, driven by thriving economic hubs, strategic infrastructure investments, and an increasing appetite among investors for luxury real estate. Cities such as Lagos, Abuja, and Port Harcourt, alongside emerging satellite towns, are experiencing a boom in construction characterised by rising rental prices, contemporary architectural designs, and waterfront developments that appeal to high-net-worth individuals (HNWIs) and corporate clients. Developers in the market are adopting increasingly strategic approaches to capture market demand. Structured instalment payment plans, outright property sales, and the creation of residential and mixed-use development zones are now common tactics to attract investors. High-net-worth individuals, both local and diaspora, remain crucial funding sources for high-end real estate projects, and developers often prioritise private wealth investors over commercial financing to execute their ventures efficiently. Areas such as Ikoyi, Eko-Atlantic City, Oniru, Banana Island, Lekki, Victoria Island, Trans Amadi, Peter Odili Road, Rivtaf Golf Estate, and Lekki Garden Estate in Port Harcourt are at the forefront of luxury developments. In Abuja, neighbourhoods like Jabi, Katampe, Asokoro, and Maitama continue to host premium homes, while satellite areas like Lugbe, Kubwa, Gwagwalada, and Kuje in Abuja, as well as Epe and Ibeju-Lekki in Lagos, are emerging as more affordable alternatives for mid-income property investors priced out of central districts. Secondary cities such as Asaba in Delta State, Uyo, the capital of Akwa Ibom State, and selected urban areas in Oyo and Ogun states are also increasingly attracting investor attention, driven by lower land costs and emerging economic activities. High-end apartments and estates enjoy a stable degree of occupancy, often supported by corporate leasing arrangements. Developments typically feature high-rise towers, twin towers, penthouses, maisonettes, and duplexes, catering to clients who seek both status and long-term value. Luxury developments and investment trends A recent report by Estate Intel, a leading real estate data firm, revealed that up to 753 apartments priced at $1 million or above are under construction in Lagos alone. Most of these units are concentrated in Old Ikoyi, Eko-Atlant
summary: The Lagos real estate market is experiencing a surge in demand for high-end properties, driven by economic hubs, infrastructure investments, and investor appetite, which may lead to increased competition for Mixta Africa in the premium segment.

mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: The article may impact our costs and pricing strategies, as we may need to adjust our offerings to remain competitive in the premium segment.

title: Lekki Phase I leads Lagos shortlet market with ₦93.78bn revenue in 2025
description: Lekki Phase I leads Lagos shortlet market with ₦93.78bn revenue in 2025  nigeriahousingmarket.com
content: Lekki Phase I dominates Lagos short-term rental market with ₦93.78bn revenue housinglatest local news Mar 16 Written By Ayomide Fiyinfunoluwa Lekki Phase I emerges as Lagos’ highest-earning shortlet hub in 2025 Lekki Phase I emerged as the highest-earning short-let submarket in Lagos in 2025, generating ₦93.78 billion in revenue, according to the Lagos Shortlet Market Report 2025 released by Edala Development. The report tracked 6,398 short-term rental listings across Lagos, with more than 1,000 units located in Lekki Phase I, reinforcing the district’s position as a major hub for short-stay accommodation in the city. Industry analysts attribute the strong performance to high demand from business travellers, tourists, and diaspora visitors seeking flexible accommodation options in prime urban locations. Occupancy rates and pricing trends The report shows that short-let apartments in Lekki Phase I maintained an average occupancy rate of about 66% during 2025, with daily rental rates averaging ₦226,000 across different property types. Occupancy rates varied throughout the year, rising from 47% in January to a peak of 77% in April, before reaching 85% by December, reflecting seasonal demand patterns in Lagos’ hospitality market. Pricing also varied significantly depending on property size and luxury level. For example: One-bedroom apartments averaged about ₦111,000 per night Five-bedroom villas reached approximately ₦377,000 per night Short stays dominated booking patterns, with 56% of bookings lasting a single night and 29% lasting two nights, highlighting the market’s focus on short-term travellers. Revenue performance across Lagos submarkets While Lekki Phase I generated the highest revenue, other Lagos districts also recorded strong performance in the short-let sector. According to the report: Lekki Peninsula II generated ₦72.02 billion in 2025 revenue Ikoyi recorded ₦35.99 billion Victoria Island earned ₦34.81 billion Ikeja generated ₦22.94 billion However, some areas experienced declining revenues. Banana Island’s earnings dropped to ₦9 billion, while Surulere recorded ₦6.15 billion, reflecting changing demand patterns and increased competition within the market. Lagos shortlet market reaches ₦281bn Overall, Lagos’ short-let rental sector generated ₦281.03 billion in total revenue in 2025, up from ₦264.3 billion recorded in 2024, indicating continued expansion in the city’s alternative accommodation market. The sector’s growth has been driven by several factors, including: rising demand from corporate travellers increasing tourism activity growing interest from digital nomads property investors seeking higher rental yields Real estate analysts note that short-term rentals often provide significantly higher returns compared with traditional long-term leases. Emerging trends in Lagos’ short-stay market The report highlights several evolving trends shaping the market. Demand is expanding beyond traditional prime districts such as Lekki, Ikoyi, an

title: Real estate firm unveils 232 housing units
description: Real estate firm unveils 232 housing units  The Guardian Nigeria News
content: Real estate firm unveils 232 housing units By : Sunday Aikulola Date: 4 March 2026 3:17am WAT Share : Follow Us on Google Discover Executive Director,Gafford Property and Homes, Dr Akinwale Ogundipe (left)Managing DIrector, Gafford property and Homes,Omo'ba Gbadebo Olamerun; Chief Ajaguno, Olori Oba Onipede, his imperial highest Oba Musiliu,Afolabi Kasali Olipede Olu of Orile Itori Oke Egba Land and Dr. Executive Director, wilson Akpan, at the client appreciation and award night of Gafford Property and Homes Limited Executive Director,Gafford Property and Homes, Dr Akinwale Ogundipe (left)Managing DIrector, Gafford property and Homes,Omo'ba Gbadebo Olamerun; Chief Ajaguno, Olori Oba Onipede, his imperial highest Oba Musiliu,Afolabi Kasali Olipede Olu of Orile Itori Oke Egba Land and Dr. Executive Director, wilson Akpan, at the client appreciation and award night of Gafford Property and Homes Limited ‎Targets growth as property demand surges Gafford Property and Homes has unveiled plans to deliver 232 housing units across Lagos and Ogun States, signalling its transition into full-scale property development amid rising housing demand and widening supply gaps in Nigeria’s real estate market. ‎ The Managing Director/Chief Executive Officer, Gbadebo Olamerun, revealed the expansion plan at the company’s Client Appreciation and Award Night, where he reaffirmed its commitment to structured communities, transparency and long-term value for investors. ‎ The projects include 100 four-bedroom bungalows at Elerangbe, Ibeju-Lekki; 32 housing units at Abraham Adesanya, Lagos; and another 100 bungalows at Iro Town, Papalanto, Ogun State, a combined 232 units aimed at tapping growing residential demand along the Lagos–Ogun growth corridor. ‎ The expansion comes at a time Nigeria faces an estimated housing deficit of over 17 million units, according to data from the Federal Mortgage Bank of Nigeria, with rapid urbanisation in Lagos and neighbouring Ogun intensifying pressure on available housing stock. ‎ Industry analysts estimate that Nigeria requires at least 700,000 new housing units yearly to bridge the deficit, yet current supply falls significantly short of this benchmark. Lagos alone accounts for a substantial share of unmet demand, driven by population growth, migration, and rising middle-income housing needs. ‎ Recent data from the National Bureau of Statistics show that the real estate sector remains a key contributor to GDP growth, supported by private sector-led estate developments and infrastructure expansion in emerging corridors such as Ibeju-Lekki. ‎ Analysts say developers shifting from speculative land sales to structured housing projects reflect a maturing market, as buyers increasingly prioritise serviced estates, legal certainty, and long-term value appreciation. ‎ Speaking further at the event, the Managing Director/Chief Executive Officer, Gafford Property and Homes Limited, Olamerun, said the company’s achievements were driven by collabora
summary: The unveiling of 232 housing units by Gafford Property and Homes signals a surge in property demand in Lagos, potentially pressuring Mixta Africa to accelerate new launches and premium product offerings to maintain market share.

title: Dennis Isong: When Is the Right Time to Buy Land?
description: Dennis Isong: When Is the Right Time to Buy Land?  BellaNaija
content: Land is more than soil and space. It represents security, progress, and the hope of a stable future. Every year, new estates appear, new roads stretch into communities, and property prices adjust to each city’s constant growth. Because of this steady movement, many people find themselves asking when the right time is to buy land. Some people fear prices will rise beyond their reach if they delay. Others worry about rushing into a purchase at the wrong time. Both concerns are understandable. Real estate decisions are rarely simple, especially in cities. The truth is that timing in property investment is not only about the market. It is also about personal readiness, financial stability, and long-term vision. Understanding these elements can help anyone decide calmly whether to move forward now or exercise patience. Several years ago, a businessman who lived in Port Harcourt contacted me about buying land in Lagos. At that time, prices in a fast-developing corridor still felt affordable to him. He liked the location and had the funds available, but he hesitated. He decided to wait one more year, hoping prices might reduce or a better opportunity might appear. Instead, development in that area accelerated. Roads improved, new estates opened, and demand increased. By the time he returned, the same plots had become significantly more expensive. He eventually bought land in a nearby location, but he often mentioned that his biggest regret was not the price he paid later. His regret was the time he lost while waiting for certainty that never came. Waiting sometimes protects you, but waiting too long can also cost you. Understanding How Land Prices Behave It is important to understand how land values typically move, especially for a place like Lagos. Unlike some markets where prices rise and fall sharply, Lagos land prices often increase gradually over time, especially in areas with growing infrastructure. Population growth plays a major role. More people continue moving into Lagos for work, education, and business opportunities. As the population increases, the demand for housing and land naturally rises. This steady demand rarely allows prices to remain low for long periods. Infrastructure development also shapes land value. New roads, bridges, schools, and commercial centres often transform quiet locations into busy residential communities. Once this transformation begins, prices usually adjust upward rather than downward. While small fluctuations can occur, a long-term decline in well-positioned Lagos land is uncommon. When Buying Now Makes Sense There are moments when purchasing land immediately is the wiser choice. One of those moments is when a buyer is financially prepared and has found a safe, well-documented property in a location with visible growth. Delaying in such a situation may not provide a real advantage. Instead, it may expose the buyer to rising prices or reduced availability. Land, unlike many other investments, is limited in supply.
summary: The article underscores persistent buyer interest in Lagos land, especially in fast‑developing corridors where prices are still perceived as affordable, reinforcing confidence in continued demand for premium and commercial parcels. This sentiment supports Mixta Africa’s focus on higher‑margin products and new‑sales mix shift.

mixta_relevance.direct_impact: Accelerated buyer interest aligns with Mixta’s premium Lakowe Lakes offerings and LNT commercial plots, aiding the shift toward higher‑margin sales.
mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: Positive sentiment may lift land price expectations, influencing pricing strategy and potentially tightening financing terms for buyers.
mixta_relevance.strategic_opportunity: Leverage the diaspora ‘Own It 4 Sure’ channel to capture motivated investors seeking timely land acquisition, expanding bulk transaction pipelines.
mixta_relevance.risk_flag: Increased speculative buying could create short‑term price volatility and pressure on receivable collection cycles.

title: Africa Real Estate Market Size, Share & Growth Report, 2034
description: Africa Real Estate Market Size, Share & Growth Report, 2034  Market Data Forecast
content: Last updated: February, 2026 Africa Real Estate Market Size, Share, Trends & Growth Forecast Report By Property (Residential, Commercial, Industrial, Land, Others), Type (Sales, Rental, Lease), and Country (Sudan, Egypt, Kenya, Ethiopia, Ghana, South Africa, Rest of Africa) – Industry Analysis, 2026 to 2034 The African real estate market reached USD 233.51 billion in 2025, is forecast to grow to USD 244.04 billion in 2026, and is expected to reach USD 347.31 billion by 2034, at a CAGR of 4.51% from 2026 to 2034. Real estate is marked by high informality, fragmented land tenure systems, and varying levels of regulatory maturity. This transition is reshaping real estate dynamics in cities like Lagos, Nairobi, Cape Town, and Cairo. International investors have also become more active in the market by collaborating with local developers to address the increasing demand for formal housing and commercial real estate. Moreover, the rise of smart city initiatives such as Egypt’s New Administrative Capital and Kenya’s Konza Technopolis is setting new benchmarks for urban planning and sustainability. Fintech-enabled property finance, growing digitization, and cross-border capital inflows are all contributing to the African real estate market's growing transition from unstructured transactions to transparent, organized, and investor-friendly frameworks that promote long-term value development. Africa is experiencing one of the fastest urbanization rates globally, which is driving the growth of the real estate market. Urban migration is further accelerating this trend, as young people move to cities in search of employment and better living standards. According to the Lagos Metropolitan Area Transport Authority (LAMATA), Lagos alone welcomes almost 300,000 new residents each year. This surge in urban dwellers is increasing demand for affordable housing, commercial offices, and retail spaces, especially in secondary cities like Kumasi, Kigali, and Lusaka. Governments and private developers are responding by launching integrated housing schemes and mixed-use developments. For instance, Rwanda’s "City Tower" project in Kigali aims to provide both residential and office space in a single complex, which caters to the growing middle class. Foreign direct investment has emerged as a significant driver of Africa's real estate market expansion, especially in the luxury and commercial sectors. Investors from China, the Gulf States, and Europe are increasingly viewing African cities as emerging hubs for commerce and innovation. In South Africa, Johannesburg and Cape Town have attracted substantial investment in green-certified office buildings and logistics parks. Governments are facilitating these investments through policy reforms, tax incentives, and public-private partnerships. Global investors looking for return and diversity are finding Africa's real estate market increasingly attractive due to increased governance and transparency. Lack of easily available mortg
summary: The Africa real estate market is expected to grow at a CAGR of 4.51% from 2026 to 2034, driven by increasing demand for formal housing and commercial real estate, which is expected to have a positive impact on the Lagos real estate market, particularly in the premium and commercial segments.

mixta_relevance.direct_impact: This growth is expected to support our strategic priority of shifting new-sales mix toward higher-margin / faster-converting segments, particularly premium Lakowe Lakes products.
mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: The increasing demand for formal housing and commercial real estate may also lead to increased competition in the market, affecting pricing and costs.
mixta_relevance.strategic_opportunity: The growth of the Africa real estate market presents a strategic opportunity for Mixta Africa to pursue institutional / bulk transactions and corporate mortgage programmes, including the MREIF/MOFI diaspora and domestic mortgage partnership with Access Bank.
mixta_relevance.risk_flag: The increasing competition in the market may also pose a risk to Mixta Africa's execution of its strategic priorities.

title: Harmony Gardens surpasses $1bn valuation with Lekki Expansion
description: Harmony Gardens surpasses $1bn valuation with Lekki Expansion  Vanguard News
content: Harmony Garden & Estate Development Limited has announced a major milestone, expanding its activities across the Ibeju-Lekki corridor and pushing the projected long-term value of its estate portfolio beyond $1 billion. Under the leadership of Chief Executive Officer Hon. Dr. Audullahi Saheed Mosadoluwa, the company is developing seven estates along the rapidly growing Lekki–Ibeju axis. The portfolio spans land assets and ongoing residential construction across key high-growth locations. A centerpiece of the expansion is Lekki Aviation Town, located near the proposed Lekki International Airport and internally valued at over $250 million. The development blends modern urban living with neighborhood charm and forms part of the company’s phased growth strategy in the region. Other estates under development in the corridor, collectively referred to as the “Citadel of Joy,” include Granville Estate, Majestic Bay Estate, The Parliament Phase I & II, and Harmony Casa Phase I & II. With multiple projects underway, the launch of the Ibile Traditional Mortgage System, and a structured expansion plan, Harmony Garden & Estate Development Ltd is solidifying its footprint in Nigeria’s fast-growing Ibeju-Lekki real estate market, reinforcing its position as a leading property developer in the region. Comments expressed here do not reflect the opinions of Vanguard newspapers or any employee thereof.
summary: Harmony Gardens' $1bn valuation and expansion into the Ibeju‑Lekki corridor signals a surge in premium residential development, intensifying competition for high‑margin projects in Lagos. The move underscores the growing attractiveness of the Lekki‑Ibeju axis for institutional investors and could reshape land pricing dynamics.

mixta_relevance.direct_impact: Heightened competition for premium Lakowe Lakes units and LNT commercial plots as Harmony Gardens targets the same high‑margin buyer pool.
mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: Potential upward pressure on land acquisition costs in Lekki/Ibeju, and increased scrutiny on zoning and infrastructure approvals.
mixta_relevance.strategic_opportunity: Leverage Mixta's institutional financing and diaspora sales channel to differentiate premium offerings and consider joint‑venture or land‑swap arrangements with Harmony Gardens.
mixta_relevance.risk_flag: Risk of eroding market share in the premium segment and compressing margins if land price escalation outpaces revenue targets.

title: Harmony expands estate developments across Ibeju-Lekki
description: Harmony expands estate developments across Ibeju-Lekki  The Sun Nigeria
content: Harmony expands estate developments across Ibeju-Lekki 24 February 2026 8:35 am WAT By sunnews Follow Us on Google Harmony Garden & Estate Development Limited has expanded its development activities across Ibeju-Lekki, pushing the projected long-term value of its estate portfolio beyond $1 billion. Led by Chief Executive Officer Hon. Dr. Audullahi Saheed Mosadoluwa, popularly know Saheed Ibile, the company is developing seven estates within the Lekki–Ibeju corridor. Details available on Harmony Garden & Estate Development show a portfolio spanning land assets and ongoing residential construction across key growth locations. A major component is Lekki Aviation Town, where urban living meets neighborhood charm, located near the proposed Lekki International Airport and valued internally at over $250 million. The development forms part of the company’s broader phased expansion strategy within the axis. Other estates in the corridor tagged as the “Citadel of Joy” (Ogba-idunnu) include Granville Estate, Majestic Bay Estate, The Parliament Phase I & II, and Harmony Casa Phase I & II. With multiple projects active, the rollout of the Ibile Traditional Mortgage System, and structured expansion underway, Harmony Garden & Estate Development Ltd continues to deepen its presence within the fast-growing Ibeju-Lekki real estate market. summary: Harmony Garden & Estate Development's expansion in Ibeju-Lekki signifies increased competition in Lagos' premium and mid-market segments, potentially pressuring Mixta Africa's pricing and sales mix.

mixta_relevance.direct_impact: Harmony's expansion may pressure Mixta Africa's sales mix towards more affordable segments, affecting our strategic priority of shifting new-sales mix toward higher-margin segments.
mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: Increased competition in Ibeju-Lekki may lead to downward pressure on pricing, affecting our revenue targets.
mixta_relevance.strategic_opportunity: Mixta Africa can capitalize on its existing relationships with institutional investors and bulk buyers to secure deals in Ibeju-Lekki, potentially creating a strategic opportunity.
mixta_relevance.risk_flag: Harmony's expansion increases the risk of execution delays and cost overruns in our active projects, particularly Lakowe Crossings and Lakowe Annexe.

title: Nigeria’s housing crisis stems from policy failures, says Ezekiel
description: Nigeria’s housing crisis stems from policy failures, says Ezekiel  Business News Nigeria
content: Share Nigeria’s housing deficit, estimated at over 20 million units, is often blamed on private developers. However, Ryan Ezekiel, Founder of Zeeks Homes, says policy and structural failures limit the ability of developers to deliver homes at scale and at prices most Nigerians can afford. Speaking on the issue, he pointed to the high Monetary Policy Rate (MPR) as a key obstacle to sustained building activity. “The MPR policy is a major stumbling block to developers building consistently,” he said. He said most projects are funded out of pocket because developers cannot afford bank financing, which raises risk and the cost of capital. He added that the lack of affordable long-term finance pushes developers away from rental housing that requires capital to be tied down for years. “We see less rental properties because huge capital cannot be locked down in properties for a long period of time to generate consistent cash flow, making the cost of real estate inaccessible to the middle class,” Ezekiel noted. “If developers could finance projects long term, there would be no need to demand outright payment for properties and this would further reduce the entry level as a home buyer.” He said the absence of a functional mortgage system means many buyers must pay the full price upfront, keeping ownership beyond the reach of middle and low income households. “Mass housing projects have become cash cows for government individuals and parastatals,” he stated. “Having a subsidized mass housing scheme where developers get large lands at little to no cost and they build affordable housing for the middle class and low-income earners would go a long way. Right now, the cost of land is too high so the prices of houses are way higher than they should cost. More effectively designed PPPs would go a long way in solving this problem.” He also cited land administration and approval processes that rely on manual systems, leading to delays that increase project timelines and costs. Frequent demolitions in Lagos and Abuja and changing regulations reduce investor confidence and make long-term planning difficult. Nigeria sliding back to Abacha era, but we will defeat them — Gbenga Hashim Nigeria's laser skin industry faces growing safety crisis Ebube Immanuel receives recognition at Yessiey Awards He said multiple agencies impose overlapping levies despite recent tax changes that clarified some VAT rules, while enforcement remains weak. “Governments need to fix our fiscal policy problems and lower inflation rate,” he said. “This will make building materials affordable and reduce the cost of building low-cost durable housing for the middle class and low-income earners.” He said transparent public-private partnerships would restore investor confidence and unlock large-scale affordable housing supply. “More first-time home buyers and a huge cut in our housing deficit,” he concluded. His remarks add to calls for coordinated policy reform to address financing, land access and re
summary: Nigeria's housing crisis is driven by policy failures, particularly the high Monetary Policy Rate, which hinders developers' ability to deliver affordable housing, posing a significant threat to Lagos' real estate market.

mixta_relevance.direct_impact: This affects our strategic priority of shifting new-sales mix toward higher-margin / faster-converting segments, as the lack of affordable long-term finance pushes developers away from rental housing.
mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: The high Monetary Policy Rate may increase our costs and affect our pricing strategy, while the lack of affordable long-term finance may impact our ability to attract mid-market buyers.
mixta_relevance.strategic_opportunity: We can capitalize on the opportunity to develop more premium products, which are showing stronger documented returns than affordable tier.
mixta_relevance.risk_flag: The risk of execution is high due to the policy failures and structural issues in the industry.

title: Housing deficit in Nigeria driven by policy failures, not developers – Ryan Ezekiel
description: Housing deficit in Nigeria driven by policy failures, not developers – Ryan Ezekiel  Vanguard News
content: Housing deficit in Nigeria driven by policy failures, not developers – Ryan Ezekiel  Vanguard News
summary: The article attributes Nigeria's chronic housing deficit to policy shortcomings—land allocation, financing and mortgage frameworks—rather than a lack of developer capacity. For Lagos, this underscores persistent supply constraints, especially in the affordable segment, while highlighting the need for regulatory reforms to unlock higher‑margin development.

mixta_relevance.direct_impact: Policy bottlenecks could delay approvals and financing for Lakowe Crossings and LNT commercial plots, potentially slowing revenue contribution toward the 2026 target.
mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: Higher land and financing costs may compress margins on affordable projects; regulatory uncertainty could affect DFI credibility and bulk‑transaction pipelines.
mixta_relevance.strategic_opportunity: Mixta's focus on premium Lakowe Lakes products and its diaspora sales channel positions it to capture demand once mortgage reforms improve credit access, offering a competitive edge over developers reliant on low‑margin affordable units.
mixta_relevance.risk_flag: If policy reforms stall, project timelines and cash‑flow for LNT scaling could be jeopardized, raising execution risk for the 200,000‑resident target.
